title: Chapter 25: The Second Kiss
content: Two weeks since Rowan walked back into the studio at three in the morning, and we have built ourselves a careful rhythm.

He brings coffee in the morning — leaves it on the corner of my worktable, near my hand but not touching my hand. He sits in the reading chair on the far side of the studio, the one with the cracked leather arm that sags when you lean into it, and he reads while I work. Sometimes he reads aloud — just soft fragments, passages from whatever novel he's carrying that week — and his voice becomes the texture of the afternoon, wool-warm and low, filling the space without crowding it.
He has not touched me once.
Not once in fourteen days.

He holds doors open and then steps back. He passes me scissors handle-first and withdraws his fingers before mine arrive. When we walk through the city together he keeps a full arm's length between us on the sidewalk, and I can tell it costs him because his left hand keeps twitching toward me — a small, aborted gesture he probably thinks I don't notice. I notice everything about him now. I notice the way he breathes when he's concentrating. I notice the particular angle of his jaw when he's trying not to say something.
He's respecting the rules I set.
And I watch him from across the studio, my hands still on the fabric, and I think: the rules were supposed to protect me. Instead they've become a cage we're both living in.

The studio lamp hums above me — its constant low drone, the sound I've worked beside for decades, the sound that means I'm here, I'm making something, I'm still alive. Tonight the hum of the lamp and the hum beneath my skin are two different frequencies, and I can hold them apart, and that is the only reason I'm still functioning.
I turn back to the dress form.
The 1743 dress.
I have been trying to make this dress for three hundred years.

The pattern was wrong — always wrong. The side seams curved too sharply, the bodice pulled at the waist where it shouldn't, the darts sat a millimeter off from where they needed to be. I would cut the fabric and sew the seams and stand the form up and look at it and something would be wrong. Always one thing — one crucial, maddening thing — that kept the dress from being what I remembered.
But tonight the pattern is right.

I don't know what changed. Maybe it's the rhythm we've built. Maybe it's the careful, chosen distance. Maybe grief just took three hundred years to resolve into something my hands could translate. Maybe I finally stopped trying to remember Venice and started making what I actually felt.

I pin the last seam. The silk slides through my fingers — midnight blue, the color of the lagoon after sunset, the color of the sky over San Marco during a storm — and the pin goes in clean, and the fabric settles against the form, and I step back.
The dress is there.
Complete.

It glows in the studio light like a ghost made fabric. Three centuries of grief compressed into midnight-blue silk, and the bodice fits like a hand closing around a throat, and the skirt falls in deep pleats that remember wind off the water, and the neckline — the neckline is exactly as it was in 1743, wide and low, made for a woman who believed she had all the time in the world.
My knees buckle.
I catch myself on the edge of the worktable and the silk is still under my fingertips and it's done, it's finally done, and Marco is still gone.
The crying starts before I can stop it.

Not delicate crying. Not the performative grief of a woman in a film, lovely tears tracking down porcelain cheeks. This is ugly — gasping, shoulder-heaving, snot and salt and centuries of suppressed sorrow pouring out of me like water from a cracked dam. I'm making sounds I've never made, animal sounds, wretched and raw. My vision blurs. My chest heaves. The dress stands there on its form, perfect and finished, accusing me with its completeness.

Because finishing it means admitting I'll never wear it for him. Means admitting the dress outlived the man. Means admitting I spent three hundred years trying to sew my way back to a moment that's gone, that's been gone since before the United States existed, that will never come again no matter how many times I cut and pin and stitch.
I taste salt on my lips. My tears. My mouth is full of it — the ocean of Venice, the salt of three hundred years of weeping I never allowed myself.
Rowan is beside me instantly.
He doesn't touch me. He remembers the rules.

But he's there — close enough that I can feel the warmth radiating off his skin without contact, close enough that his presence becomes a pressure against my side like a hand held an inch above water. He's breathing deliberately, slowly, and I can hear it, and that rhythm grounds me even as I fall apart.
"Tell me what this dress is," he says.
Not *are you okay*. Not *what's wrong*. He knows me better than that now. He knows the grief is about the thing, not around it. He asks the right question and it cracks me open further.

"It's Marco's," I say, and the name hurts to say — it hurts like pressing on a bruise I've been carrying for centuries. "Marco. Venice. 1743. He was —" I stop. Breathe. My chest is heaving. "He was the first person who saw me. The real me. Not the woman, not the shape, just — me. And I was making this dress for him. For him to see me in. And he died before I finished it."
The words are coming faster now, tumbling out, and I can't stop them and I don't want to.

"He was a sailor. He was supposed to come back from a run to Corfu. Three weeks, he said. Three weeks and he'd be home." My voice breaks. "The ship went down in a storm off the Dalmatian coast. All hands lost. I read about it in the Gazette — a single paragraph, his whole life reduced to ink on cheap paper. And the dress was sitting here, half-finished, and I couldn't complete it because completing it meant —"
I can't finish the sentence. But I don't have to. Rowan understands.
Completing it meant he was never coming back.

"I've been making it for three hundred years," I whisper. "The same dress. Over and over. Never getting it right. And it's done now and he's still gone." I'm sobbing again, doubled over, my hands braced on my knees. "He's still gone. Three hundred years and he's still gone and I don't know how to — I don't know how to carry it anymore."

I look at Rowan through the blur of tears and his hand is hovering near my shoulder. Wanting to touch. Not touching. His jaw is tight and his eyes are bright and I can see the effort it costs him to hold back, to honor my rules even while I'm falling apart in front of him, even while every cell in his body must be screaming at him to reach for me.
And I think: the cage. We're both in the cage. And I'm the one who locked it.
I turn to face him.

My eyes are red and swollen. My face is a wreck. I don't care. I don't care about anything except the truth of this moment and the man standing in front of me who has spent two weeks not touching me because I asked him not to, and it's killing us both.
"I'm going to kiss you now," I say.
He goes very still.
"And I need you to know that I'm choosing this. Not the hum. Not the curse. Me. This is me. Orlando. Choosing you."
I reach for his face with both hands.
My fingers find his jaw — rough with the day's stubble, warm, real — and I pull him to me and I kiss him.

Not brief this time. Not the startled, accidental kiss of weeks ago. This kiss is deliberate. Long. Deep. Desperate and tender at once, like a prayer and a confession in the same breath. I pour everything into it — the dress, the centuries, the salt on my tongue, the grief and the wanting and the terrifying, exhilarating choice of reaching for another person when reaching has always meant losing.
The hum surges.

It roars through me like a wave, rising from somewhere beneath my ribs, and I feel the shift pressing against the inside of my skin — the thing I've been holding back my entire life, the transformation that comes when emotion overwhelms control. The hum wants out. The shift wants to happen. My body is vibrating with the effort of containing it.
And I hold it.

I hold it because this is worth the risk. Because his mouth is on mine and he tastes like Bordeaux — the wine he had with dinner, still lingering on his tongue — and like warmth and like the specific, irreplaceable scent of Rowan, and I will not lose this moment. I will not let the curse take it from me.
Rowan's arms go around me.

He lifts me off the ground — actually lifts me, one arm around my waist, the other hand cradling the back of my head — and I wrap my arms around his neck and the kiss deepens and I am crying into his mouth and he is kissing the tears off my lips and the kiss tastes like salt and Bordeaux and three hundred years of loneliness ending.

I'm suspended. In the air. In the moment. In the arms of a man who came back and didn't touch me for two weeks because I asked him not to and who is holding me now like I'm the most precious thing he's ever been trusted with.
The hum screams.
I hold.
The shift claws at the inside of my skin.
I hold.
I hold because he's worth it. Because I'm worth it. Because three hundred years of grief is enough, and I am done letting fear write the rules.
We break apart.
My feet touch the ground and his hands settle on my waist and we're both breathing hard and then I see his face change.
His eyes widen.
He's looking at my arms. I look down.
My skin is shimmering.

Actually shimmering — visible in the warm studio light, a faint luminous ripple across my forearms and up my neck, like heat haze above asphalt in July, like the surface of water when something moves beneath it. The shift didn't happen, but it tried. It pressed against the boundaries of my body and I held it and it's retreating now, but the evidence is there, written in light on my skin.
"Is that —" he starts.
"Yes." My voice is shaking. "It's the shift. It's starting."

I'm terrified. The hum is still there, coiled beneath my sternum, pulsing with readiness. The shimmer is fading but slowly, the light withdrawing back beneath my skin like a tide going out, and I don't know what would have happened if I'd let go, if I hadn't held, if his kiss had pushed me one degree further —
But his hands are steady on my waist.

His grip hasn't loosened. His eyes are wide but they're not afraid. He's looking at me — at the shimmer, at the impossible thing my body just did — and his expression isn't horror or fascination. It's something closer to reverence. Like he's standing at the edge of something vast and choosing not to step back.
"Do you want me to stop?" he asks.

The most respectful question anyone has ever asked me. Not *what's happening to you*. Not *are you okay*. Not *what are you*. Just — do you want me to stop? Giving me the choice. Always giving me the choice.
I shake my head.

The shimmering subsides. The last ripples of light sink back under my skin, and I'm just Orlando again — tear-streaked, swollen-eyed, standing in the studio with a man's hands on my waist and a finished dress on the form behind me and three hundred years of grief howling somewhere in the background, quieter than it's ever been.

The hum settles. Not to silence — never silence — but to a low, warm vibration. Not a warning. Not yet. Just readiness. A held breath. The hum is waiting, and I am choosing, and for the first time in centuries those two things don't feel like enemies.
Rowan's thumb traces a small circle on my hip. A tiny, deliberate gesture. Contact. Chosen contact.
We stand there. The studio lamp hums its drone. The midnight-blue dress glows on its form. And somewhere in the distance, in a part of myself I've kept locked away since 1743, a door opens.
I hear the sharp intake of breath from the doorway before I see her.
Suki is standing there, frozen.

She's carrying two takeout bags — our dinner, probably, the Thai place on West 29th she always brings when she knows I've been working too long. Her keys are still in her other hand. She must have let herself in with the spare we gave her months ago.
She's not looking at the dress.

She's looking at me. At my arms, where the last ghost of shimmer is fading. At Rowan's hands, still on my waist. At the two of us standing in the warm studio light, close enough that our breath mingles, glowing — *actually glowing* — with something that has no explanation in any world she's ever lived in.
Her lips part. No sound comes out.
The takeout bags rustle in her grip.

I can see the calculation happening behind her eyes — the desperate, rational part of her brain trying to file what she just saw under *trick of the light* or *reflection* or *anything, please anything besides what it looked like*. But Suki is a costume designer. She works with light and fabric and the way things appear when you manipulate them. She knows what she saw.

My skin was shimmering. The secret I've kept for four hundred years, the thing I've hidden from everyone I've ever loved, just announced itself in front of the one person I never wanted to hurt with it.

And Rowan's hands are still steady on my waist, and the hum is humming its low warm note, and the 1743 dress is finished on its form, and Suki is standing in the doorway with dinner going cold in her hands, and the look on her face is the look of someone whose world just cracked open and is trying very hard not to fall in.
I don't know what to say.
I don't know how to explain.
But I know this: the second kiss happened, and the shift almost happened, and Rowan didn't let go.
And Suki just saw everything.
